f74deae0b5a1:
Villagers only take fright at enemies that can actually hurt them. Scanning for
any enemy at all meant a single enemy medic -- who does no damage and outruns
spearmen -- could stop an entire economy by standing next to it, and effectively
warned the victim before the units that could kill them arrived.
Military units deliberately do not use this filter: an army should target medics.
e4f30e95b226:
Two things wake a villager: an armed enemy inside three tiles, or being hit
(including a neighbour shouting after being hit).
The radius is derived from weapon ranges rather than picked flat, and three tiles
puts it just inside every ranged unit's reach: archers, longbows and catapults
all get one free shot before the villagers react, while horse archers fall just
outside and have to close the distance -- raiding on mobility rather than range.
The second trigger is not optional: without it a catapult sitting outside the
alert radius killed seven of eight villagers in forty seconds without any of
them reacting at all.
e2fee08f2f12:
Natural resource nodes seat a limited number of gatherers, and the seats are
shared across factions -- both sides compete for the same node. Finite nodes are
what force expansion and fights over ground.
Renewable buildings (farms, coppices, fish traps) stay one villager each.
A seat is held for the whole round trip, so nobody is queue-jumped mid-haul.
ad2065f9bb38:
Seat counts are recomputed per call, which costs O(villagers) and cannot go
stale.
The earlier version had the free-seat check scan the villagers itself, and it was
called inside a per-node loop -- O(nodes x villagers). Since every forest tile is
a node, late-game maps have thousands of nodes and hundreds of villagers, and a
single simulation step went from 2.7ms to 41ms.
b8171c53c09e:
Repair scan: damaged, safe, finished buildings get villagers assigned
automatically (repair is an extension of building), and siege engines get one
villager each.
477998ff4f31: Flee target: the nearest finished friendly building with room to garrison.
5e769ef84f69:
Valid targets for fighting back: enemies inside our vision (including any
exposed by their own fire) that are attacking us or a villager nearby, nearest
first. "Do not chase too far" is defined by vision -- what cannot be seen is not
followed.
1a44e2635771:
A building counts as besieged when an enemy is within 240px, which is what
locks automatic repair out of danger.
82b82de545eb:
Emergency repair target: a damaged, besieged, still-standing friendly building,
nearest and within 420px -- nobody is pulled across the map to die.
Where garrisoning is also possible, hysteresis on the building decides: above
75% HP villagers stay hidden, below 40% they come out to repair, so they do not
oscillate in and out of the door.
5d2c354919ee: Nearest finished friendly building, used as cover for idle villagers.
a074d964645c:
Fleeing moves one step directly away from the weighted sum of nearby enemies,
rather than towards a building -- which is what stopped villagers running to a
town centre they could not enter and standing there being shot.
When a wall, cliff or map edge blocks that direction, it widens the search angle
and takes the walkable direction that still moves furthest from the enemy.
Returns true while still fleeing.
90ff5f6d6d91:
Stance resolution when not repairing and not hiding: fight back = attack the
nearest threat, flee = retreat, keep working = carry on.
da21b6a9455f:
One-shot nudge: dragging a unit walks it to where you let go, then clears --
and it goes back to whatever it was doing. It works on every unit type as a
safety valve for getting something unstuck.
One at a time, no box select: no reward for clicking speed, so it never becomes
micromanagement. It is safe on military units too, because "go back to what you
were doing" walks them back to their group, so nobody can hand-place a formation
unit by unit with it.
220dca101219:
Villager behaviour, arbitrated: emergency repair (critical) > garrison / bell >
stance.
985d2b8e8394:
The two triggers again: an armed enemy within four tiles, or having been hit
recently (including a shout from a neighbour). The second one looks back over a
wider 340px to find the threat, so that a catapult with 235 range is not only
noticed but locatable -- you have to know which way to run.
e7c4600ce0b9:
Already on the way to cover means not giving up halfway. Without this check a
villager that got 240px from the enemy was sent back to idle, stopped in the
open and never reached the town centre at all -- garrison counts peaked at zero.
Coming back out is the building's job (safe for three seconds, then released),
and the work time lost on that trip is exactly what a raid should cost.
793ba56af144:
Full, or nothing to garrison in: with a threat, fall back to the stance (fight,
flee or keep working); without one, carry on working.
ad1ce604683e:
Contact test for construction: normal buildings within radius + 22 of the
centre, docks (which sit in water) as soon as the villager stands on a tile
adjacent to the footprint -- otherwise a villager on the shore is just outside
the radius, cannot reach the dock and stands there idle. Both require the same
height level: you cannot build up a cliff from below.
341bc430cd21:
Repairs cost resources: a full repair is 35% of the build cost, scaled by how
much damage there is. Buildings with their own repair basis use it -- the capital
and cities are free at game start or arrive by upgrade, so their rebuild cost is
not their repair cost.
bc7063d80390:
Gatherers stand on a ring around the node, spread by the golden angle from their
id, so several villagers on one node each walk to their own spot instead of
colliding on whoever arrived first.
Arrival uses two thresholds: approaching aims tightly at your own ring point,
while already-gathering accepts a loose radius, so being jostled does not knock
anyone off the job.
72464273747c:
Fishing boats are villagers on water: gather the nearest fishery, haul to the
dock, flee when threatened. They neither build nor fight.
64037669b8b1:
Fishing-boat flight: the villager algorithm on water and at boat speed. Legality
keeps it in its own movement domain, blocked directions widen the search angle,
and direction hysteresis stops the jitter. It keeps retreating until nothing is
within 300px, and does not return to the fishery on its own.
2bda42c96aa1:
Ring positions for boats, matching villagers. Boats have a body radius of 9, so
two hulls sit at least 18px apart -- but the old code required being within 16px
of the node centre to count as fishing, so the third and fourth boat on a
fishery were pushed out of gathering range by separation every tick, repathed,
and were pushed out again. Over a minute the fourth boat travelled 1092px
without gathering anything.
The fix is one golden-angle ring point per boat at the separation radius, two
arrival thresholds to stop the jitter, and a jam valve for an occupied point.
222776fc9e7a:
Terrain integrity lives in these two checks: placement, shoving, straight-line
destinations and close-quarters interaction all go through them.
3f553a41af98:
Melee cannot reach across a cliff -- more than half a level of difference
forbids it, a ramp's half level does not. Ranged fire is unaffected.
cd65b90f4931:
Focus fire: the group votes on a shared target. Ranged units vote for every
enemy in range, melee for the nearest, and the most-voted wins with the lowest
HP breaking ties. Recomputed every 0.4s with hysteresis so it does not flicker.
3632a54253f0:
Return fire: being attacked by a visible enemy locks onto it even outside the
engagement radius -- which is what guard and hold postures need most. The chase
is still bounded by the order's leash, and the unit returns to its post
afterwards.
01118fde2f5f:
Walking around units that are standing still. Before each step the unit looks a
short way ahead: if someone stationary is in the way, it takes the first legal
direction to one side that does not run into another stationary unit -- it slides
past. If every direction is blocked (a wall of people in a gap) it falls back to
pushing through at 45% speed. Shoving works, but it has resistance, and the
natural path is the one without any.
It does not avoid its own attack target, moving units (two movers simply give way
to each other), or anything within a step of its destination.
00b03c25a7f2:
Cliff guard: if a frame's movement would cross more than half a level, the path
has been corner-cut or shoved off course, so it repaths rather than walking over
a cliff. A unit that finds itself inside a blocked tile -- built over, or spawned
inside a building -- is allowed out to any unblocked tile.
19839027702f:
Target weighting for the counter rule. It decides *who* to shoot, never how much
damage is done; damage comes from the bonus table.
The default borrows the damage multipliers as scores: units that shoot back rank
above towers, which rank above other buildings, so incoming damage drops first.
Siege units carry a building bonus and therefore still prefer buildings. On the
easiest difficulty the rule is simply "nearest", with no ordering at all.
Lookup order: buildings check the raid category (farms, coppices, fish traps)
first, then the building type; non-buildings check villager, then unit type;
anything unlisted falls back to the default.
4a17dbbcbd5c:
Melee and ranged defence (buildings and villagers have none; siege splash
ignores defence entirely).
5ec7af2594e1:
Siege engines need setup time: a unit with a setup value has to stand still for
that long before it can fire, and any movement restarts the count.
It keys off the last-moved timestamp rather than its own state, because only
real movement writes that timestamp -- being shoved by a friendly unit does not,
so physical jostling never interrupts a setup, and firing while relocating stops
working automatically without a check in every branch.
Only the first shot after repositioning is affected: once set up the cooldown is
unchanged, so setup costs mobility, not damage output.
693965eb8fae:
Medics: never attack, automatically heal the worst-wounded living unit nearby,
and back off when an enemy closes in.
0b314d0f038a:
Candidates are wounded living units within seek range, ordered by lowest HP
fraction, then by distance.
60cc523cb34d:
============ Patrol zones (friendly buildings, single-linkage clustering,
recomputed whenever one is built or lost) ============
b541baf65507:
Kiting stands between the enemy's range and ours, biased towards the enemy.
Deliberately not at the very edge of our own range: sitting at the boundary makes
units drift in and out of it, which amplifies tiny timing differences into win-
rate differences. It also leaves the opponent a way to close -- this is kiting,
not an unreachable treadmill.
6da6f3543ca8:
A siege engine being closed down first looks for another target it can still
reach and keeps firing -- let the friendly units deal with whatever is in its
face. Only when the attacker is the last thing left does it retreat. Standing
firm also means not moving, so the setup is never interrupted.
6b5d9949b59d:
The chase leash is anchored to the ordered point (attack orders get more rope
than guard), which stops a group chasing one runner all the way into the enemy
base. A target the player picked explicitly is the exception: that is a
commitment, and it is followed however far it goes.
c3b75b8bd7b8:
Shoving has resistance: the unit that is moving absorbs most of the separation,
the one standing still gives way only slightly. Total displacement is unchanged,
so separation is as fast as it ever was.
3aff0c269a98:
Find a legal, unblocked spot at the same height around a building -- so units
leaving a garrison do not end up stuck or on the wrong level.
4703ba89a985:
A minimum stay, applied only to automatic release (manual eviction and a
critical building always empty immediately).
Automatic release triggers when nothing has been near *the building* for three
seconds -- but a raid hits the outlying resources, so with the enemy far from the
town centre that timer is already satisfied and villagers were released the same
tick they entered. Garrison counts peaked at zero and it looked like they could
not get in, when in fact they were being let straight back out, walking back to
the node, and being shot again.
c724ba584829:
Transports are garrisons that move: capacity is the sum of unit volumes, loading
and unloading crosses the land/water boundary, and a partial unload is fine when
there is not enough room ashore.
edf7e8692e3f:
Rendezvous for loading. A transport heads for the shore water nearest the units
waiting for it and stops once they are in loading range, rather than continuing
to the far bank -- which is how a transport and its passengers used to end up on
opposite sides of a dock forever. It only re-approaches while idle, so even a
crossing start converges instead of orbiting.
3dde267fa40b:
Single exit point for cancelling an ambush. Once ambush is set there should be
no follow-up input, so anything that touches the group or its members -- moving,
targeting, dragging, transferring, emptying a garrison, changing formation --
releases it back to guard. If the group panel is open it is redrawn on the spot,
so the ambush highlight cannot linger and suggest it is still armed.
bae79cc92901:
Units already garrisoned are left alone, so a group can be split across several
towers.
43a72c6177c5:
Emptying a garrison, at group level: every member of the group inside a building
comes out. The player does it from the group panel; the AI calls the same
function rather than getting a private one.
It also clears the hold order, so "come out" really means out -- otherwise newly
trained members of the group would walk straight back in.
c0020f6e8dda:
The per-unit garrison order has been retired. It was the no-UI version of
dragging a single unit into a building, and the player has no such gesture --
which made it a capability the AI had and the player did not. Garrisoning is a
group-level order for both, and the AI command whitelist has no entry that the
player cannot press.
f6226d5c936e:
Buildings shoot by real vision, like everything else: tree cover accumulates per
tile, so a tower sees the treeline but not the depths, and a unit exposed by its
own fire can be hit. The omniscience shortcut does not see through trees either,
so towers never snipe an ambush hidden in deep forest.
This used to differ by side -- one side's towers fired at remembered buildings,
the other's only at currently visible ones -- which was a third source of side
bias. Now both go through the same rule: remembered is enough for buildings,
currently visible is required for units.
2ef51e6bdad6:
A garrison adds arrows: archers count 3, everything else 1, and every 3 points
adds one arrow.
28349156d554:
Projectiles turn at a limited rate instead of homing, so a fast target that
changes direction sharply can make an arrow miss. Dodging by movement stays a
property of what you can see happening, and neither the evasion nor the damage
model is involved.
aaa2f46471db:
Evasion: a moving unit has a speed-dependent chance to dodge an arrow (3% while
standing). Buildings are always hit. Ranged defence is subtracted after a hit.
44e9ca58eff3:
Splash falls off with distance: the unit at the point of impact takes full
damage, and it drops linearly to 40% at the edge of the blast. That is the real
benefit of a loose formation -- spread units are mostly at the edge of the blast
rather than simply outside the radius. Buildings take no falloff, which keeps
siege-versus-wall balance where it was.
3dd4f1176543: ============ 8b. Scouts (mode-driven units, outside the group system) ============
8345fa9fc58b:
Safety ring: the range of enemy defensive buildings this side has actually seen,
plus a buffer -- a tower nobody has laid eyes on is not avoided by clairvoyance.
Both "enemy" and "seen" are relative to the scout's own side; the old code had
them hard-coded, so one side's scouts avoided their own towers and ignored the
opponent's.
11f08ca035a9:
Once the map is explored, scouts roam: pick a random explored, walkable tile
outside any known defensive fire and wander to it. This replaced standing at the
enemy's door, which either got the scout killed or parked it somewhere it could
see nothing, and it keeps map-wide intelligence fresh without ever walking into
fire that is already known about. Taking a deliberate look at the enemy base is
a separate, explicitly dispatched scout.
